blocks.text: If a man divorces his wife and she goes from him and becomes another man's wife, will he return to her? Would not such a land be greatly polluted? You have played the whore with many lovers; and would you return to me? says the Lord.

1 : The rhetorical question demands a negative answer. Deut 24.1–4 prohibits second marriages to the same person. To or turn, repent, is repeated several times in the larger unit (e.g., vv. 7,10,12,14,22; 4.1; cf. Deut 4.30; 30.1–2,10). Although the analogy would exclude the possibility of repentance and reconciliation between people and God, later passages in the chapter envision such restoration.

Look up to the bare heights, and see! Where have you not been lain with? By the waysides you have sat waiting for lovers, like a nomad in the wilderness. You have polluted the land with your whoring and wickedness.

2 : , the MT contains two alternate readings of the verb. One (Heb “shagal”) is probably vulgar, meaning to have sex with or to be raped. Ancient editors preferred to read a passive form of “shakab,” “lain with.”
Therefore the showers have been withheld, and the spring rain has not come; yet you have the forehead of a whore, you refuse to be ashamed.
3 : Israel's behavior pollutes the environment and disrupts nature; see Hag 1.5–11. , i.e., stubbornness (cf. Isa 48.4).
Have you not just now called to me, “My Father, you are the friend of my youth—
4 : , Heb “ʾalluph” implies God's intimacy.
will he be angry forever, will he be indignant to the end?” This is how you have spoken, but you have done all the evil that you could.
3.1–5: No return possible. Jeremiah uses an abbreviated form of the Deuteronomic law on marriage and divorce in the charges against Israel.
The Lord said to me in the days of King Josiah: Have you seen what she did, that faithless one, Israel, how she went up on every high hill and under every green tree, and played the whore there?

6 : One of few specific references other than genealogical to (640–609 ) in Jeremiah 1–25 (also 1.2; 25.3; see also 22.15–16). , high places were sites for illicit worship, and the wood pole/tree represented the goddess Asherah (see 2.27n.)
And I thought, “After she has done all this she will return to me”; but she did not return, and her false sister Judah saw it.
She saw that for all the adulteries of that faithless one, Israel, I had sent her away with a decree of divorce; yet her false sister Judah did not fear, but she too went and played the whore.
8 : , see Deut 24.1–4.
Because she took her whoredom so lightly, she polluted the land, committing adultery with stone and tree.
9 : , a priestly term for contaminating the sacred (see also 3.1,2; Lev 19.29; Num 35.33–34).
Yet for all this her false sister Judah did not return to me with her whole heart, but only in pretense, says the Lord.
Then the Lord said to me: Faithless Israel has shown herself less guilty than false Judah.
3.6–11: Israel and Judah's continued unfaithfulness. Exilic-era prose continues the broken-marriage metaphor and argues that Judah is guiltier than Israel.
Go, and proclaim these words toward the north, and say: Return, faithless Israel, says the Lord. I will not look on you in anger, for I am merciful, says the Lord; I will not be angry forever.
12 : , the kingdom of Israel, north of Judah, had earlier been carried away by the Assyrians into captivity. What follows is directed to the Northern Kingdom.
Only acknowledge your guilt, that you have rebelled against the Lord your God, and scattered your favors among strangers under every green tree, and have not obeyed my voice, says the Lord.
13 : , implying a dangerous and defiling foreignness.
Return, O faithless children, says the Lord, for I am your master; I will take you, one from a city and two from a family, and I will bring you to Zion.
3.12–14: Call for Israel's return.
14 : , the metaphor shifts from promiscuous spouse to disobedient children. (Heb “baʿalti bakem”), the Heb word “baʿal” also means husband; see 2.23n.
I will give you shepherds after my own heart, who will feed you with knowledge and understanding.
15 : , kings and leaders. , lit., “shepherd you.” , a description reminiscent of David (1 Sam 13.14).

And when you have multiplied and increased in the land, in those days, says the Lord, they shall no longer say, “The ark of the covenant of the Lord.” It shall not come to mind, or be remembered, or missed; nor shall another one be made.

16 : , Judah's central religious symbol may have been removed in a military attack and no longer in the Temple by Jeremiah's time. Later tradition credited Jeremiah with hiding the Ark following the destruction of the Temple (2 Macc 2.4–5) on Mount Nebo, the site of Moses's secret burial (Deut 34.1,6).

At that time Jerusalem shall be called the throne of the Lord, and all nations shall gather to it, to the presence of the Lord in Jerusalem, and they shall no longer stubbornly follow their own evil will.
17 : As part of the divine throne, the ark was God's footstool (1 Chr 28.2; Ps 132.7–8); now itself will become God's .
In those days the house of Judah shall join the house of Israel, and together they shall come from the land of the north to the land that I gave your ancestors for a heritage.
3.15–18: Israel's restoration.
18 : , both Assyria and Babylon were symbolically located in the north; see 1.14n.

I thought how I would set you among my children, and give you a pleasant land, the most beautiful heritage of all the nations. And I thought you would call me, My Father, and would not turn from following me.

19 : Addressed to the young woman. Normally women did not inherit property, as illustrated by the case of the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 26.33; 27.1–7; 36.2–9; cf. also Job 42.15). , cf. Ps 106.24; Zech 7.14. , see 3.4.
Instead, as a faithless wife leaves her husband, so you have been faithless to me, O house of Israel, says the Lord.
A voice on the bare heights is heard, the plaintive weeping of Israel's children, because they have perverted their way, they have forgotten the Lord their God:
21 : , hot, dry, barren land, scene of Israel's misery (see 14.6).
Return, O faithless children, I will heal your faithlessness. “Here we come to you; for you are the Lord our God.
Truly the hills are a delusion, the orgies on the mountains. Truly in the Lord our God is the salvation of Israel.
23 : (Heb “sheqer”), a play on code language for the Canaanite god Baal. is a strong translation; Heb “hamon” implies only the tumultuous noise of a crowd out of control.
“But from our youth the shameful thing has devoured all for which our ancestors had labored, their flocks and their herds, their sons and their daughters.
24 : , a reference to Baal; see 2.26n. , on voracious divine appetites see 51.33–44; cf. Bel 1–28.

Let us lie down in our shame, and let our dishonor cover us; for we have sinned against the Lord our God, we and our ancestors, from our youth even to this day; and we have not obeyed the voice of the Lord our God.”

2.1–4.4: Divine grief over Israel's infidelity. This unit uses intertextual links, shifts in (Israel's) gender identity, dissonant voices, and contrasting emotions, as well as dialogue, satire, exhortation, and legal precedent to imagine God as a spurned lover longing for reunion with God's loved one. The metaphor of rejected lover unifies the larger context (see also Hos 1–3; Isa 1.21; Ezek 16; 23).
3.19–4.4: God's plea for Israel's return. A return to the motif of God's prodigal children and promiscuous spouse to speak of Israel's apostasy and God's longing for reconciliation.
25 : As Israel lay with Baal (3.2), so now they will in their (see v. 24) as the consequence mirrors the offense.
